summary: Amjad Masad 说，Apple 50 周年已经变成了一场 PR 灾难；Aaron Levie 则认为，agents 只会带来更多工作，不会裁员。Rauch 把 engineers 直接推到 customer hot seat 上，Claude 也提醒团队得赶紧把 security 做扎实。

builderInsights.title: Amjad Masad
builderInsights.suggestedTitle: Apple 50周年，正变成一场公关灾难
builderInsights.suggestedSummary: 他认为，Apple 在 50 岁生日这天，正朝着“全球最招人恨的公司”这个方向走。这是 Replit CEO 对 Apple 眼下糟糕应对方式的一记直球。

tweets.text: On its 50th birthday Apple is doing its best to become the most hated company in the world. https://t.co/9JCgiiCrBo

builderInsights.authorBio: ceo @box - your business lives in content. unleash it with AI
builderInsights.title: Aaron Levie
builderInsights.suggestedTitle: Agents 不会干掉工作岗位——它们会带来更多工作
builderInsights.suggestedSummary: 他说，enterprise AI 正在从 chat 转向真正能执行工作的 agents。但真正的瓶颈不是模型本身，而是 change management、legacy systems 和 compute budgets。核心意思是：公司用 agents 不是为了替代人，而是为了把以前没法合理化的工作做出来。这也意味着 engineers 会变成搭建和运维 automation layer 的人。

tweets.text:
Another week on the road meeting with a couple dozen IT and AI leaders from large enterprises across banking, media, retail, healthcare, consulting, tech, and sports, to discuss agents in the enterprise. 

Some quick takeaways:

* Clear that we’re moving from chat era of AI to agents that use tools, process data, and start to execute real work in the enterprise. Complementing this, enterprises are often evolving from “let a thousand flowers bloom” approach to adoption to targeted automation efforts applied to specific areas of work and workflow.

* Change management still will remain one of the biggest topics for enterprises. Most workflows aren’t setup to just drop agents directly in, and enterprises will need a ton of help to drive these efforts (both internally and from partners). One company has a head of AI in every business unit that roles up to a central team, just to keep all the functions coordinated.

* Tokenmaxxing! Most companies operate with very strict OpEx budgets get locked in for the year ahead, so they’re going through very real trade-off discussions right now on how to budget for tokens. One company recently had an idea for a “shark tank” style way of pitching for compute budget. Others are trying to figure out how to ration compute to the best use-cases internally through some hierarchy of needs (my words not theirs).

* Fixing fragmented and legacy systems remain a huge priority right now. Most enterprises are dealing with decades of either on-prem systems or systems they moved to the cloud but that still haven’t been modernized in any meaningful way. This means agents can’t easily tap into these data sources in a unified way yet, so companies are focused on how they modernize these.

* Most companies are *not* talking about replacing jobs due to agents. The major use-cases for agents are things that the company wasn’t able to do before or couldn’t prioritize. Software upgrades, automating back office processes that were constraining other workflows, processing large amounts of documents to get new business or client insights, and so on. More emphasis on ways to make money vs. cut costs.

* Headless software dominated my conversations. Enterprises need to be able to ensure all of their software works across any set of agents they choose. They will kick out vendors that don’t make this technically or economically easy.

* Clear sense that it can be hard to standardize on anything right now given how fast things are moving. Blessing and a curse of the innovation curve right now - no one wants to get stuck in a paradigm that locks them into the wrong architecture. One other result of this is that companies realize they’re in a multi-agent world, which means that interoperability becomes paramount across systems.

* Unanimous sense that everyone is working more than ever before. AI is not causing anyone to do less work right now, and similar to Silicon Valley people feel their teams are the busiest they’ve ever been.

One final meta observation not called out explicitly. It seems that despite Silicon Valley’s sense that AI has made hard things easy, the most powerful ways to use agents is more “technical” than prior eras of software. Skills, MCP, CLIs, etc. may be simple concepts for tech, but in the real world these are all esoteric concepts that will require technical people to help bring to life in the enterprise. 

This both means diffusion will take real work and time, but also everyone’s estimation of engineering jobs is totally off. Engineers may not be “writing” software, but they will certainly be the ones to setup and operate the systems that actually automate most work in the enterprise.

Security another great example of a job category that is about to have its Jevons paradox moment as well. 

“And counterintuitively, I think better AI tooling for security will increase the demand for security talent, not decrease it. Autonomous exploitability automates the proving step, but it doesn't automate the response. More real findings surfaced faster means more triage, more remediation, more architectural decisions that need human judgment”

AI is going to generate 100X more code, and along with that, there will be an enormous increase in security discoveries. AI is the only way to triage all of these new threats and risks, but an expert still will be needed on the other side to manage the process. Going to be a massive category of opportunity for talent.

builderInsights.title: Guillermo Rauch
builderInsights.suggestedTitle: 让你的工程师直接坐到客户的“热座”上
builderInsights.suggestedSummary: 他说，提升产品最快的办法，就是把 engineering lead 直接拉进和最挑剔客户的 group chat 里，然后快速发版，正面接住反馈。Vercel 已经在 v0 上这么做了，他也在邀请愿意深度参与的用户一起加入这个闭环。

tweets.text:
Protip: Create an X group chat with your best and most demanding customers. Put your engineering leads in said group chat. Respond and ship quickly. Face the music.

We have one going for @v0. DM if you want to be a part of it and are committed to sharing excellent feedback.

builderInsights.authorBio: General Partner @SouthPkCommons, Co-Founder @Bevel_Health | Ex: Early Eng @facebook, CTO @Dropbox, Board @Flipkart | Optimist, Builder, Dad
builderInsights.title: Aditya Agarwal
builderInsights.suggestedTitle: Anthropic 很像早期的 Facebook，好的坏的都像
builderInsights.suggestedSummary: 他说 Anthropic 身上有种早期 Facebook 的味道：好玩、天真、自下而上的 hacker culture，还有很强的 mission 对齐。问题也在这儿——他们可能有点太把自己当回事了。不过只要一直赢，这些毛病通常都会被盖过去。

tweets.text:
It's interesting...the Anthropic culture reminds me a lot of early Facebook....despite OpenAI having a lot more ex-Facebook people

Similarities:
--> People within the company are having a lot of fun
--> There is a lot of naivete about the world
--> There is a bottoms-up hacker culture
--> There is insane "mission" alignment
--> They might be taking themselves just a bit too seriously
--> Winning solves a lot of things

builderInsights.authorBio: President & CEO @ycombinator —Founder @garryslist—Creator of GStack & GBrain—designer/engineer who helps founders—SF Dem accelerating the boom loop
builderInsights.title: Garry Tan
builderInsights.suggestedTitle: Agentic engineering：厚技能、厚代码、薄 harness
builderInsights.suggestedSummary: 他觉得，今年最清楚的一条经验，就是按层来拆工作：模糊、需要人判断的部分，放进 markdown skills；完全确定、可重复的部分，留在 code 里；harness 则尽量保持薄。这个规则很 YC，很实用。它能帮你做 agent，又不把整套系统搞成一团意大利面。

tweets.text: New item in my SOUL md tonight https://t.co/iAwcQ2QCB7

Do the right job at the right layer 

Everything else is architecture astronomy https://t.co/jqiPf14F0B

This is the simplest distillation of what I have learned about agentic engineering this year 

Push smart fuzzy operations humans do into markdown skills. Fat skills. 

Push must-be-perfect deterministic operations into code. Fat code. 

The harness? Keep it thin. https://t.co/3ESkNepZrZ

builderInsights.authorBio: partner @fpvventures - investing in seed/A. previous: early hire @meter, @opendoor, @atlassian & others. love @shimoleejhaveri + 👦👧
builderInsights.title: Nikunj Kothari
builderInsights.suggestedTitle: 在郊区创业，不要挤进市中心
builderInsights.suggestedSummary: 他觉得，更多 founder 应该学 DoorDash：别一上来就扎进最显眼的战场。先从边缘切入，去那些通勤没那么折磨人、城市竞争没那么卷的地方。这个判断是，郊区 startup hub 以及围绕它们的 coworking、infra 和公司，还会继续长大，尤其对更有经验的 builder 来说更是这样。

tweets.text:
I'm as SF-pilled as they come.. but more people should adopt the "Doordash" strategy for building companies. 

Doordash famously won the race against Uber Eats because they focused on suburban markets right outside cities. While the main players were all focused on the main cities, Doordash started with suburbs and then ate into the main markets. 

Similarly, I think we'll see more companies started by people that are wildly ambitious, that are close to large cities but are tired of doing the 20-30 minute commute every day (Palo Alto is a good example). You can argue that @Waymo makes commuting not an issue, but people still don't want to sit in cars. 

When I was at @meter, I saw a lot plans of coworking spots that were starting in suburbs to fulfill this demand and I think that'll continue to happen vs. competing in hyper competitive cities. 

This will skew to more experienced people than the 19 year old who wants to live in SF/NY/LA and that's perfectly fine. There will be markets for both. 

If folks have tried this already, I'd love to learn how it's going!

builderInsights.title: Zara Zhang
builderInsights.suggestedTitle: 做了个 Marie Kondo 风格的 tab 清理器
builderInsights.suggestedSummary: 她开源了一个 Chrome 新标签页，专门治 tab 乱成一团的问题：按 domain 分组，一键清理重复 tab，批量关闭先处理最容易的，还能把要晚点看的内容先存进 checklist。最有意思的是细节打磨——关 tab 时会有 swoosh 音效和 confetti——让它不只是个工具，更像一个小型 productivity game。

tweets.text:
PSA: You can vibe code your own "New tab" page in Chrome. I have turned mine into the ultimate solution to the "too many tabs" problem

- See all your tabs with clear titles, grouped by domain
- Closing any tab gives you "swoosh" sound and confetti effect 🎊
- "Easy wins" grouped together: homepages, localhost tabs... batch-close them with one click
- Duplicate tabs detected; close duplicates with one click
- For tabs you're not done with, save it for later in a checklist

This is the Marie Kondo method for browser tabs

Open-sourced the code below

podcastHighlights.title: Marc Andreessen 反思 The Death of the Browser、Pi + OpenClaw，以及为什么“这次不一样”
podcastHighlights.suggestedTitle: AI 热潮是真的，但基础设施扩张还是可能跑在需求前面
podcastHighlights.suggestedSummary:
**核心结论：** AI 不是那种常见意义上的泡沫。它更像是一个酝酿了 80 年的 research stack，终于开始在真实产品里兑现。

- 反直觉的点在这：大家一提“this time is different”通常就会警惕，但这次不一样的地方是，核心赌注已经在现实世界里跑通了。
- 真正的风险不是 AI 不再进步，而是公司把 infrastructure 建得太快，市场还没来得及消化。很像 2000 年的 telecom。
- 麻烦主要在 adoption，不在 capability：model 可能一路猛进，但 institutions、regulation 和人的行为都很慢，而且很不均匀。

Andreessen Horowitz 联合创始人 Marc Andreessen 把这波 AI 浪潮看成是一次迟到很久的兑现，而不是突然冒出来的奇迹。他把时间线往前拉到 1943 年的最早 neural network 工作、Dartmouth 时代的雄心、1980 年代的 expert systems 热潮、2013 年的 AlexNet、2017 年的 transformers，再到最近这一串 LLM、reasoning models、agents 和 self-improvement。他的判断很直接：这是一次“**eighty year overnight success**”。

在他看来，变化不只是 model 变大了。更关键的是，怀疑论者最强的几条论据，已经被一条条打穿了。先是 LLM 看起来像高级 autocomplete。接着 reasoning models 证明它们真能做实事。然后 coding 在最难落地的场景里把这件事坐实了。现在，agents 和 automated research 又把边界往前推了一截。所以他说，“**now it’s working**”。

但 Andreessen 同样关注第二层问题：capital allocation。他提醒，AI infrastructure 还是可能重演 dot-com 时代的错误——如果所有人都默认 demand 会永远翻倍。只是这次不一样的是，最大买单方是 Microsoft、Amazon、Google、Meta、Nvidia、OpenAI 和 Anthropic，不是那些资本薄得可怜的 telecom startup。至少现在，这些投入都能立刻被消化掉，因为 compute 还是稀缺的。真正的问题不是 AI 重不重要，而是谁在为下一代 model 建东西时，不会先被它压垮。

blogUpdates.title: 为 AI 加速攻击做安全准备
blogUpdates.suggestedTitle: Claude 呼吁围绕补丁、检测和 IR 强化 AI 时代的安全防护
blogUpdates.suggestedSummary:
**重点：**Claude 认为 AI 正在压缩 exploit 的时间窗口，建议安全策略来一次重置：更快打补丁、自动化 triage、用前沿模型扫描 code，并把系统设计成默认会被攻破。

**数据：**
- 这篇文章警告说，在接下来的 **24 months** 内，AI 可能会发现并串联起“海量 bug”并把它们变成 exploit。
- 面向 Internet 的 app，一旦 exploit 可用，应该在 **24 hours** 内完成 patch。
- 安全团队要按 **一个数量级** 的增长来规划 vulnerability 发现量。

**所以：**
对 builder 来说，核心就是从手工、靠 spreadsheet 的安全流程，切到自动化、model-assisted 的工作流。重点包括：用 CISA KEV 和 EPSS 缩小 patch gap，持续扫描 dependency 和 code，用 SLSA 加强 build provenance，以及通过 zero trust、短生命周期 token 和硬件绑定身份来缩小 blast radius。Claude 也强调要把 AI 放进流程里："If you implement one thing from this section, implement this" —— 指的就是 AI vulnerability scanning 这一节。实际做法很直接：用 AI 找 bug、做 triage、起草修复方案、在攻击者之前跑 red-team 式检查，同时让人类保留最终的 containment 和 disclosure 决策权。
